X5.1.label: Question X5.1
X5.1.topic: Embedded value calculations under Solvency II, MCEV, and analysis of surplus.
X5.1.synopsis: Embedded value options under Solvency II / MCEV: Free surplus, VIF, RM and SCR. Discussion on continuing to publish EV, identifying MCEV components, impact of marketing campaign, estimating sale price, and differences between short-term analysis of profit and long-term analysis of surplus.
X5.1.scenario: A proprietary health and care insurance company writes a range of long-term health and care insurance products. The company is based in a country that is about to adopt a solvency regime that is identical to Solvency II.

(i).question: Explain whether or not the VIF element should be included in the company's calculation of the embedded value under Solvency II.

Inclusion of VIF in EV: Therefore there may be differences between the Solvency II and EV discount rates ... [½]
... and so profits (or losses) will emerge over time that should be included in the EV calculation. [½]
Future profits can only be taken credit for up to any contract boundaries under Solvency II. [1]
In its EV, the company might wish to hold an element of VIF in relation to future profits arising beyond such boundaries, if they apply. [½]

This may be the case if it sells flexible premium unit-linked business and may not be able to recognise profits in respect of expected, non-contractual future premiums on in-force policies under Solvency II. [1]
Therefore, it would be appropriate to include VIF in the calculation. [½]

(ii).question: Discuss the factors the company should consider in deciding whether to continue to publish embedded value results in future.

Publishing EV in future: The company should consider how many other companies are not publishing EV results. [½]
If most companies are not going to publish EVs, it strengthens the argument for not doing so ... [½]
... although the circumstances and underlying business of different companies will be different, so there are valid reasons for them to reach different conclusions. [1]

An important factor will be the other information that the company is publishing and intends to publish and how well this other information informs the shareholders, analysts, etc of the company's performance. [1]

Previously, the EV may have been useful if the regulatory reports and accounts did not show the VIF. This would mean investors would not have any information on the profitability of new business written over the year. [½]
However, under Solvency II, the sale of profitable new business is reflected, and so stakeholders will get this information. [½]
All companies will be complying with Solvency II disclosure requirements. [½]

How well these disclosures reflect the company's view and performance depends on, for example, the extent to which the results are constrained by Solvency II rules, eg in recognising returns in excess of risk-free rate and premiums beyond contract boundaries. [1]
So, companies that have, for example, significant annuity business may have a stronger argument to publish EV results. [½]
The incentive to publish EV results may also depend on the company's risk appetite and the value measure used within the company. [½]
If management decisions are made based on an EV measure, then publishing those results may be helpful. [½]
The decision will also depend on what information the company is providing in its primary accounts. [½]

For example, if the company is in a country that has continued to publish prudent reserves in its statutory accounts, it may feel the accounting information it is publishing is not as indicative of the company's performance as it would like ... [1]
... whereas if the country has moved to a more fair-value approach (such as IFRS17) the company may see less value in continuing to publish EVs as well. [½]
There is a significant cost to performing multiple sets of reporting in terms of staff time and expertise, systems requirements, audit controls and processes, etc. [1]
These may be made more onerous by the standards and timescales necessary for public disclosure. [½]

The tighter reporting timescales and more onerous requirements of Solvency II may themselves use additional company resources, and so may make the company less able to report on an EV basis, even if it has had sufficient resources to do so in the past. [½]
Any change, and in particular a reduction, in the company's published information will need to be justified to the users of that information. [½]
Ceasing to publish EV will reduce external analysts' ability to compare the company's performance with that of prior years. [½]
The company should consult with analysts and major shareholders to find out whether they find the EV useful and their reaction if it was withdrawn. [½]
The company should consider how to manage any transition, eg when to give notice of its intention not to publish EV, provision of a reconciliation between former EVs and the new equivalent. [1]

(iii).question: Suggest how the company should determine each of the items in the analysis of change in embedded value in future. Note: Comments specific to the EEV methodology are not required in your answer.

Expected return on free surplus: The risk-free investment return assumption at the start of the year is applied to the free surplus at the start of the year. [1]
The free surplus under Solvency II is assets less the BEL less the RM less the SCR. [½]
Return on in-force business - expected r: This is the risk-free rate applied to the opening VIF. [½]
It is sometimes called the 'unwind of the discount rate'. [½]
It represents the value of the expected future profits being closer in time at the end of the analysis period, and thus being discounted by less and so having a higher value. [1]
The size of this element is likely to be small given the small VIF under Solvency II. [1]
Return on in-force business - experience: The basic approach to determining the effect of experience variances on the net assets would be to:
• Roll forward the assets backing the BEL and the BEL to the end of the year using the beginning of the year BEL assumptions as the expected experience over the year. [½]
• As this roll forward is performed using best estimates of the experience, the assets and the BEL should still be equal at the end of the year. [½]
• Repeat the roll forward process but change one of the items of experience from the expected value to its actual value. [1]
• The change in the surplus at the end of the year gives the contribution from the item of experience. [½]
• Repeat this process changing each item of experience in turn to identify its contribution to the change in net assets. [½]
Under this approach the experience variance from each source depends on the order in which each source is analysed. [½]
The process for performing this analysis is the same as that carried out for the analysis of surplus, ie starting from the opening position and changing one item at a time. [½]
The company will also need to consider the impact of experience variances on the other components of the EV. [½]
If actual experience is different from expected, then the VIF, RM and SCR components of the EV will also change. [1]
So, an experience variance item will also be required for any difference in the actual releases of profit and capital to those expected. [½]
Return on in-force business - operating: These will merge if the BEL assumptions have changed [½]
There will be separate operating assumption changes to consider for the EV experience (projection) basis. [1]
Assumptions underlying the risk margin or capital requirement calculations might also change (eg correlation coefficients), which could also impact the EV. [1]
The assumed 'cost of capital' factor might also change (ie the parameters used to determine the cost of holding the RM and SCR), the impact of which would also need to be analysed. [½]
New business contribution: This is calculated as an increase in VIF plus an increase in net assets at the point of sale (assuming the new business is written on terms that are expected to be profitable). [1]
This could be assessed by simply taking the difference between the EV with and without the inclusion of model points that relate to new business written over the year. [1]
Investment return variances: In respect of the own funds, this item will include the difference in the change in assets backing the own funds over and above the assumed risk-free returns. [1]
Another component will be the impact of the assets moving differently to the movement in BEL. [½]
The actual return must be analysed against the risk-free rate plus any matching adjustment or volatility adjustment. [1]
There may also be an impact on the cost of holding the risk margin and additional capital. [½]
For example, the capital requirements may change due to actual investment returns being different from expected. [½]
In addition, there could be a contribution to the investment return variance if the risk-free yield curve moves differently to the asset yields. [½]

Investment return variance on the VIF will reflect the actual investment return differing from the discount rate being used to discount the future profits being taken credit for in this component. [½]
Effect of currency movements: This will include economic variances resulting from differences between actual and expected currency exchange rates. [½]
Effect of economic assumption changes: This will be determined in a very similar way to the investment return variance but will consider the impact of changing the assumptions for future economic experience (whereas the investment return variance considered the impact of having different economic experience than expected last year). [½]

It may be that some of these changes are combined with investment return variances, due to the close relationship between actual economic outcome over the year and the best estimate future economic assumptions, particularly in relation to yields and bond value movements. [1]

(iv).question: Assess how this marketing campaign might impact the current and future reported embedded value, including how the impacts would be classified in the analysis of change.

Impact on the EV of the marketing campai: There will only be any impact if the company's expectation that the marketing campaign will significantly boost sales is borne out in reality. [1]
There will be no impact on the current EV as that does not take account of new business. [1]
Assuming that the business is normally written to be profitable on a best estimate basis, as would be expected to be the case, the future EV would be higher than otherwise. [1]
This will increase the new business contribution in the analysis of change in EV ... [½]
... although the effect of the marketing campaign specifically would not be separately identified. [½]
An increase in sales will not directly affect in-force business experience. [½]
However, if future new business is expected to increase then the company may reduce the per-policy expense assumptions. [1]
... and would come through in the analysis as an operating assumption change. [½]
It is unlikely that there will be a material impact on the experience variances, ... [½]
... although a small expense surplus may occur due to higher policy numbers to cover fixed expenses. [½]
It is possible that the future mix of business will change, ... [½]
... for example, the average policy size and the demographic mix may change, ... [½]
... but it will take some while before the full impact on the target market will be known. [½]
The cost of the marketing campaign will be included in the EV. [½]
The marketing campaign will cost money, pushing down assets which will show up in the EV. [½]
This may be treated as an exceptional item or a development cost in the analysis. [½]
The company may have to spend on systems and staff to deal with the increased business volumes. [½]
This spending would also reduce the EV and would partially offset the expense surplus items described above. [½]

(v).question: Describe how the sale price would relate to the component of the published embedded value in respect of this block of business.

Relationship between sale price and EV f: The first thing to consider is the assets allocated in respect of the business concerned. [1]
If the two calculations were based on different assets, the values would be different for this reason. [½]

For example, if the EV were based on £100 million assets and £105 million were transferred to the new insurer, this fact alone would lead you to expect a payment in respect of the business bigger than the EV by £5 million. [½]
Consider how the sale price will be negotiated. It is likely that the purchaser and seller will both agree to use EV calculations to determine how much should be paid for the business ... [½]
... but if the sale price is agreed by some other means, it is not possible to comment on its likely size in comparison with the EV. [½]
If the EV is to be used, then there will be a need to consider the basis for the calculations. [½]
The sale basis is a question of negotiation. The purchaser (seller) will favour a basis that produces a lower (higher) EV. [1]
Whether this results in a higher or lower EV than one using the published EV basis will depend on the strength of the negotiating parties. [½]
Some of the features that may affect this are as follows:

• The sale of the business is not a 'forced' sale. The seller is therefore not in a weak position and may be able to achieve a reasonable price for the business. Following this logic, the sale price may exceed the EV. [1]

• Conversely, if the withdrawal from the market has a specific driver, eg changing capital requirements of this class of business under Solvency II, the seller may be in a weaker position and not achieve as good a price. [½]
• The published EV is market-consistent so should in principle give values achievable when selling a block of business. [1]
• The purchaser may base the negotiations on their own expense levels rather than the seller's ... [1]
... so if the sale is achieved to a very low expense company, a price higher than the EV may be achieved because of the lower expenses expected to be incurred by the purchasing company. [½]
It is likely that any such synergy would be shared. [½]
• On the other hand, the sale price will need to allow for transaction costs ... [½]
... and the costs of the new company taking the business on board. [½]
• It may be argued in negotiations that the event of selling the business will cause a surge of lapses / surrenders. [1]
The purchasing company may insist on a very cautious (ie big) allowance for lapses in the sale price. [½]
This might make the sale price lower than the published EV. [½]
• There may be an argument from the purchaser that there will be greater uncertainty regarding the experience of the business post-sale. [1]
The purchaser may therefore push for greater allowances for risk margins and required capital, resulting in a lower EV. [1]
• Another issue is that the published EV will be based on assumptions set at a single point in time each year. The sale price is a one-off that may be determined between annual reporting dates. [½]
The sale price may therefore be higher or lower because of unusual current conditions (eg unusually high or low interest rates). [1]
Overall, with factors going in different directions, it is not possible to say which of the figures will be larger. [½]

(vi).question: Explain the reasons why the insurer would want to analyse the change in surplus on its income protection insurance business over a year.

Reasons for an analysis of surplus: The insurer could use the analysis to show the financial impact of differences between the actual and expected experience. [½]

This can be used to help ensure future assumptions are set appropriately and allow for expected trends in the business, eg medical advances helping reduce claim durations as claimants can return to work earlier. [½]
As the IP insurance business is long-term, the insurer should take care not to amend the assumptions too quickly but ensure they reflect the long-term experience. [½]

The insurer could use this analysis to help set its assumptions for valuing the block of business. It could also be of use to any company considering purchasing the block of business to help set its assumptions for the business. [½]
The assumptions could be used to profit test the new business and so help to determine whether to continue to sell IP insurance. [½]
The analysis should help the insurer identify any causes for concern so that corrective action can be taken. [½]
This may help the insurer decide whether to sell the business, or whether it can be managed within the insurer. [½]
Considering trends in the analysis of experience over time can also provide the insurer with useful information to help plan its risk management and strategy for the product. [½]
The analysis of surplus will also provide information on impact of writing new business for the insurer. It may be that its new business is profitable, but its existing business is loss making. [½]
If the analysis is carried out independently of the valuation data, the insurer can use it as a check on the valuation data and the valuation process. [½]

(vii).question: Explain how an analysis of profit for a short-term health insurer selling individual PMI would differ from the analysis of surplus for a long-term health insurer.

Analysis of profit for a short-term insu: For long-term insurances, the analysis of surplus looks at the surplus arising each year throughout their lifetime. [½]
For existing business at least, there may be little chance to react to adverse experience since the premium rates are usually fixed. [½]
For PMI, there is the opportunity at renewal to change prices, product design, underwriting or claim conditions ... [½]
... and hence it is useful to analyse profit more regularly throughout the year. [½]
An analysis of surplus would normally be an annual exercise for a long-term insurer. [½]
For a PMI insurer, the company would again assess the profitability of business by yearly cohort, but would also perform quarterly analyses to establish up-to-date positions. [½]
The quarterly calculations will be fed through to the price being charged for new business, to take account of the most recent changes in claim trends. [½]
The key items to monitor are:
• claim incidence rates, ie the number of separate claims divided by the exposed to risk [½]
• average cost per claim, ie the total incurred cost of claims divided by the number of separate claims [½]
• loss ratio, ie the total incurred cost of claims divided by the relevant earned premium. [½]
In addition to these, analysis of persistency is also important, to assess the profits lost through withdrawals. [½]
Analysis of investment profit is not so important for the short-term business. [½]
Whilst the loss ratio is quick and simple to calculate, any trends in either frequency or size of claim will be hidden. [½]
Therefore, if data allows, it is more useful to monitor the first two indices. [½]
Each will be calculated for the cohorts of business under review and will be compared with the assumptions made in the original premium pricing. [½]
Cohorts by duration are often reviewed since many PMI portfolios exhibit a selection effect; ... [½]
... the loss ratio typically increases by duration, depending on the method and depth of medical underwriting at outset. [½]
Incurred claims will include allowance for the total ultimate expected claim cost and so must include all claims paid to date (whether settled or not), and all claims yet to be reported. [1]
The definition of a claim should be consistent so that trends in experience can be meaningfully analysed, and results compared to those of competitors. [1]

If no claims discount systems are in operation, it is crucial to be able to assess experience by cohort accurately and quickly, in order to fine-tune the methodology of bonus-malus and promotion / demotion scales in operation. [1]

X5.2.label: Question X5.2
X5.2.topic: Expansion strategies: Setup vs Buy, Capital requirement, Purchase price, and Other factors for PMI.
X5.2.synopsis: Advantages and disadvantages of setting up a new company vs buying a local insurer. Calculation of capital requirements for a new operation (premium, expenses, claims, investment return). Calculation of purchase price for an established insurer (free assets, liabilities, goodwill). Other factors (brand, distribution, data, risks, reinsurance, systems, competition).
X5.2.scenario: A large private medical insurance company has approached an actuarial consultancy. The insurer is based in a developed country where healthcare is provided by the State to all citizens, free at the point of use. It is considering an expansion plan to start selling PMI business in an overseas country and considering two possible strategies: Strategy 1 – Setting up a brand new company in the overseas country. Strategy 2 – Buying an established private medical insurer within that country.

(i).question: Discuss the main advantages and disadvantages to the company of each of these two strategies.

Advantages of setting up a new company: It will be easier if the insurer wishes to make use of its own knowledge, product types or systems in the new market. [½]
For example, it may wish to be the only PMI provider rating according to age, or it may have a superior claims control system. [½]
New, efficient IT systems can be installed, which could be cheaper and can be designed from scratch, eg to collect data for premium rating more effectively. [1]
There may be benefits of branding by a large, established, well-known insurer. [½]
There will not be exposure to 'baggage' from another insurer - eg out-dated IT systems or high-claiming customer base. [½]
The purchaser will not be dependent upon any existing distribution channels and will ultimately be able to hire its own, with suitable training. [1]
It may be cheaper than buying an established insurer because there is no purchase of goodwill. [½]
There may be tax benefits or other government support (depending upon the country). [½]
Disadvantages of setting up a new compan: A great deal of preparation will have to be carried out before launch, some of which may be difficult. [½]
This will include:
• developing the appropriate systems
• establishing relationships with hospitals (and other treatment providers)
• establishing distribution channels
• researching the State systems
• researching the PMI market and culture of the country, etc
• obtaining authorisation from regulatory authorities, etc. [½ for each 2 examples]
The extent of this preparation, and the cost involved, will depend on how different the market, State system, culture, etc is from the current country the insurer operates in. [1]
The salesforce, brand name, etc will need to be built up until sales reach a reasonably high level. [½]
This may all take a long time, during which period:
• competitors will be benefiting from economies of scale [½]
• significant advertising spend may be required. [½]
Local expertise is likely to be required, eg for medical underwriting. [½]
However, this could be bought in by recruiting local people, or using consultants or reinsurers. [½]
Data required for premium rating in this country may be:
• insufficient
• in the wrong format
• provide insufficient detail
• out of date. [½ for each 2 examples]
Advantages of buying a local insurer: • experts in the local market and established relationships with providers [½]
• instant access to past data to assist with premium rating. [½]
The insurer will be buying goodwill. This consists of the insurer's salesforce, brand and customer base, which will generate future sales. [½]
Depending upon the country and the insurer that we are buying, this is probably a less risky option. [½]
The new operation will be 'up and running' instantly. [½]
Possible benefits of branding by a locally-known insurer. [½]
Disadvantages of buying a local insurer: The insurer will be paying for the extra benefits outlined above. [½]
The price may not be worth the benefit that it will receive. [½]
It may also be paying a 'price' for any 'baggage', eg out-dated systems. [½]
There also might be extra costs, eg redundancies. [½]
There might be resistance to any change in working practices or to sales methods, especially with a foreigner taking over. (This is easier to overcome if a new company is established.) [½]

(ii).question: Describe the method that would be used to calculate the capital required to set up an insurance company under Strategy 1.

Calculation of capital requirements: Need projections of future premium income, expenses, claims and investment return. [1]
Use these to give projections of future revenue accounts and balance sheets, which could be used to estimate initial capital required. [½]
Use sensitivity testing and scenario testing (or stochastic modelling), to assess capital requirements in the future. [½]
The investment assumption should take account of the expected returns on the assets backing the contract and should be consistent with the inflation assumption. [1]
Because the assets are overseas, these assumptions would be different from expected domestic investment returns. [½]
Take account of any regulatory minimum capital requirements. [½]
Items of cashflow should be net of reinsurance, although details of the reinsurance should be checked, eg for solvency admissibility purposes. [½]
Premium income: • the date when the operation will start selling business
• the expected initial level of premium income
• the expected level of growth. [½ for each 2 examples]
As the operation is new, the assumed new business growth rate may not be level over the first few years. [½]
The assumed levels of premium income should be sensible in the light of the market size and the sales levels of the key PMI providers (if any) in that country. [½]
Because the operation is new, we might initially project the cashflows monthly or quarterly and then annually later, eg after five years. [½]
The cashflows in respect of expenses and claims should be projected over the same time interval. [½]
The assumed rate of new business must be consistent with the assumed level of advertising spend and the assumed profitability of the premium rates. [1]
Consider competitors' premium rates when estimating the company's market share. [½]
Also consider expected future political, economic and cultural influences on future income. [½]
Expenses: These should be split into a number of different components, such as: establishment costs, advertising costs, new business costs, servicing costs and overheads. [1]
Each of these components should be estimated as accurately as possible and therefore should be broken down further. [½]
For example, servicing costs might be split into rent, salaries, IT cost, postage, telephone, etc. [½]
Also there should be sensible links between sales volumes and expenses, eg commission should vary with business volumes. [½]
An assumption must also be made for the level of inflation to be applied to the expenses. Different rates might apply to different expenses, eg salaries and IT costs. [½]
The inflation assumption should be consistent with the assumed level of claims inflation, although claims and expenses will not necessarily be subject to the same type of inflation. [½]
Claims outgo: This could be estimated stochastically or deterministically. [½]

To estimate the claims outgo deterministically, we need to make assumptions for the loss ratio (or claim rate), the rate of run off of claims and levels of future claim inflation. Use these to estimate claim outgo in future years for a cohort of business. [1]
These estimates would vary by type of claim. [½]
The loss ratio (or claim rate) must be consistent with the assumed profitability of premium rates and the volume and mix of business written. [½]
The ratio might change over time to reflect the level of the economy or competition (insurance cycle). [½]
Allowance should be made for claims expenses (if not mentioned above). [½]
To estimate the outgo stochastically, fit distributions to each of the claim frequency and claim amount. [½]
Assumptions would be required for the distributions and the parameters involved. [½]
Running the model several thousand times would give a frequency distribution for the range of payouts, which could then be used in the cashflow model. [½]

(iii).question: Describe the method that would be used to calculate the purchase price of the PMI insurer under Strategy 2.

Calculating the purchase price: Method 1. This could consist of the size of the free assets calculated on a realistic basis plus goodwill. [½]
An adjustment would be made for any capital that must be tied up to keep the company solvent, such as minimum capital requirements (or prudence within the outstanding claims reserve). [½]
Method 2. Alternatively, a model could be built to project and then discount future dividends. [½]
This model would allow for future new business and for the 'lock in' of capital described above. [½]
We have assumed that the first method is used. Markers should give credit for either approach. [NOTE: THIS WAS A MARKERS NOTE BUT I AM INCLUDING NO SCORE FOR ACCURACY]
The consulting actuary should provide a range of possible alternatives under suitable assumptions and scenarios. [½]
The purchaser will make the decision on the ultimate price. [½]
Free assets: This would be calculated as the total market value of the assets less a realistic estimate of the liabilities. [½]
Liabilities: The liabilities would be calculated as the sum of:
• make a realistic estimate of the money needed to meet future claims that have been reported to the target company [½]
• collect as much data as possible to split the claims by claim type [½]
• allow for claims handling expenses and future claim inflation [½]
• obtain information to be able to judge the strength of the company's reserving basis and the length of time for claims to run off. [½]
The unexpired portion of premiums that have already been received, allowing for deferred acquisition costs and reinsurance premiums. [½]
We may also require an additional unexpired risk reserve if there is any evidence that the premiums charged are inadequate. [½]
This represents claims in respect of treatments that have been carried out but have not been reported to the insurer. [½]
It should be small, depending on the insurer's claims notification procedures. [½]
Goodwill: Estimate this by applying suitable claim and expense forecasts to the estimated level of premium income in each future year. [½]
Allow for expected sales volumes, mix of business, economic influences (and other relevant factors). [½]
This premium income needs to reflect new business and renewals (ie persistency must be allowed for). [½]
The present value of the future profits of this expected future business would represent the goodwill. [½]
Use a high discount rate to reflect the uncertainty over the level of future sales. [½]
Adjustments for capital: An allowance could be made to the purchase price to allow for capital tied up within the company, eg minimum capital requirements. [½]
However, for short-tailed business, such as PMI, the level of this capital is not likely to be significant. [½]

(iv).question: Suggest other factors that should be considered when deciding upon the price to offer under Strategy 2.

Other factors to consider: Markers, students might have mentioned some of these factors in part (iii), and so give them credit if they have done so. There are a number of possibilities, and so half a mark is available for each of up to 12 sensible ideas, which include:
• our experience and knowledge of the foreign market [½]
• quality of the target company's management [½]
• the amount of data available and its quality [½]
• the extent and security of reinsurance arrangements [½]
• the security and diversification of assets [½]
• the quality and age of the target company's administration and IT systems [½]
• surplus / deficit in the pension scheme [½]
• other potential buyers and the price that they will offer [½]
• our supply of capital and how the arrangement is financed [½]
• the extent to which profits can be repatriated. [½ each, maximum 6]
